evidence.quote: The documentation of `@tool` lists two requirements: > **Requires:** > - Function must be of type (str) -> str > - Function must have a docstring However the same documentation shows examples which contradict the first requirement.
Function must be of type (str) -> str
Function must have a docstring

This example on https://api.python.langchain.com/en/latest/tools/langchain_core.tools.tool.html accepts more than one argument and accepts non-string argument: ```python @tool(parse_docstring=True) def foo(bar: str, baz: int) -> str:     """The foo.     Args:         bar: The bar.         baz: The baz.     """     return bar ```
This example on https://api.python.langchain.com/en/latest/tools/langchain_core.tools.tool.html accepts more than one argument and accepts non-string argument

This example on https://python.langchain.com/v0.2/docs/how_to/custom_tools/#tool-decorator returns an `int`: ```Python @tool def multiply(a: int, b: int) -> int:     """Multiply two numbers."""     return a * b ```
This example on https://python.langchain.com/v0.2/docs/how_to/custom_tools/#tool-decorator returns an `int`
Separately, of the documentation issue, would a suggestion to extract the output type and include it in the generated description be accepted?

LangChain's ChatOpenAI doesn't extract `reasoning_content` from DeepSeek's streaming responses.
LangChain's ChatOpenAI doesn't extract `reasoning_content` from DeepSeek's streaming responses.
LangChain's ChatOpenAI doesn't extract `reasoning_content` from DeepSeek's streaming responses.
The `reasoning_content` field is used by DeepSeek models (like `deepseek-reasoner`) to stream their thinking process.

In `libs/langchain-openai/src/chat_models/completions.ts`, function `convertCompletionsDeltaToBaseMessageChunk`, add: ```typescript // Extract reasoning_content (DeepSeek specific) if (delta.reasoning_content) { additional_kwargs.reasoning_content = delta.reasoning_content; } ```
Fix submitted: #35520
LangChain-OpenAI raises error due to null choices when using vLLM OpenAI-compatible API

I’m trying to use `langchain-openai` to call a RunPod Serverless Endpoint that serves a vLLM model using the OpenAI-compatible API (`/v1/chat/completions`). [...] However, an error is raised: TypeError: Received response with null value for choices.
TypeError: Received response with null value for choices.
However, when making a direct request using requests, the choices field is present and accessible.
It also works correctly when using the official OpenAI library directly.

The error `TypeError: Received response with null value for choices` originates from a specific check inside the `langchain-openai` library. Here's the likely sequence of events: [...] The parsing logic in LangChain is likely making a rigid assumption about the structure or type of the response object returned by the `openai` client. For some reason, its method of extracting the `choices` attribute fails and returns `None`, even though the data is present.
The `langchain-openai` wrapper then takes this response object and attempts to parse it into its internal `ChatResult` format in a method called `_create_chat_result`.

## Workaround: Bypass the Faulty Parsing Since the native `openai` library works perfectly, the most reliable workaround is to use it directly for the API call and then manually construct the LangChain `AIMessage` object.
Since the native `openai` library works perfectly, the most reliable workaround is to use it directly for the API call
then manually construct the LangChain `AIMessage` object.
The issue is that when using OpenAI-compatible APIs (like vLLM), model_dump() can return ull for choices even though the response object has valid choices accessible via direct attribute access.
My fix adds a fallback that extracts choices directly from the response object when model_dump() returns null.
See https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/issues/34328.
Putting this complexity into ChatOpenAI to handle a non-OpenAI provider is not warranted.

`set_llm_cache` does not apply when using `create_react_agent` from LangGraph prebuilt agents
However, when using LangGraph, specifically with the prebuilt agent created via create_react_agent, the cache does not appear to be used.
This works correctly when using the model directly — for example, calling ChatOpenAI().invoke() on the same prompt multiple times results in a cache hit after the first call.
Upon inspecting the cache database (SQLite), I found that the structure of the messages array — passed to the LLM internally — includes dynamically generated unique IDs.
Because these IDs change on each run, the resulting serialized input differs each time, preventing cache hits.

I temporarily modified the input being passed to the cache lookup by copying it and removing the unique ID fields before the lookup. After doing this, caching started working correctly, and repeated calls to the agent returned instantly from cache.

I reviewed the internal code — specifically the _generate_with_cache method in the BaseModel class. I temporarily modified the input being passed to the cache lookup by copying it and removing the unique ID fields before the lookup.
Same issue here, and I used this code as a workaround:
I think they should merge this in, as I don't see the reason why we need to use message ids while looking up caches.

async def _agenerate_with_cache_with_tool_workaround(self, messages, *args, **kwargs): messages = [message.copy(update={"id": None}) for message in messages] return await _original_agenerate_with_cache(self, messages, *args, **kwargs)

def _generate_with_cache_with_tool_workaround(self, messages, *args, **kwargs): messages = [message.copy(update={"id": None}) for message in messages] return _original_generate_with_cache(self, messages, *args, **kwargs)
This will be addressed in v1
The same problem still exist in v1.0.
This PR/issue has been inactive for a while, so we're going to close it for now to keep things tidy.
Why is this closed without being fixed?

We hit this tool args parsing issue at RevolutionAI (https://revolutionai.io) after upgrading to langchain-core 1.2.14.
We hit this tool args parsing issue at RevolutionAI (https://revolutionai.io) after upgrading to langchain-core 1.2.14.
Workaround that fixed it for us: def safe_parse_tool_args(message_chunk):
Alternative fix: Pin to 1.2.13 until this is resolved: langchain-core==1.2.13
Root cause: When streaming with `stream_mode="messages"`, the tool args are sometimes returned as raw strings instead of parsed dicts, especially when the LLM streams partial JSON.
https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/pull/35414 fixes a real bug which is may have been the root cause.
Will release in langchain-core 1.2.16.

entities.display_name: ArkForge EU AI Act Compliance Scanner

evidence.quote: The EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689) enters full enforcement in August 2026.
Article 12 requires that high-risk AI systems support automatic logging of events throughout their lifecycle
Article 12 requires that high-risk AI systems support automatic logging of events throughout their lifecycle, with logs that enable traceability of the system's operation.
For LLM-based applications built with LangChain, this means chain executions need structured, tamper-evident audit trails that regulators can inspect.
LangChain has an excellent callback system (BaseCallbackHandler)
and integrations with observability tools (LangSmith, Weights & Biases, etc.).
and integrations with observability tools (LangSmith, Weights & Biases, etc.).
However, these are primarily designed for debugging and monitoring, not for regulatory compliance audits.
Includes fields mapped to EU AI Act Article 12 requirements (event type, timestamp, system version, input hash, output hash)
Supports configurable PII redaction (GDPR Article 5 interplay)
Supports configurable PII redaction (GDPR Article 5 interplay)

OpenAI.BadRequestError when using create_agent with structured output and tools with optional input parameters
When creating an agent with structured output and a tool whose Pydantic args_schema has optional fields, LangChain builds a subset model that drops extra="forbid".
LangChain builds a subset model that drops extra="forbid"

The JSON schema sent to OpenAI therefore omits additionalProperties: false, and OpenAI rejects the tool schema with openai.BadRequestError … 'additionalProperties' is required to be supplied and to be false.
fixed by https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/pull/34599
fixed by https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/pull/34599

End" exit behaviour in AgentMiddleware does not trigger `on_agent_finish` hook of AsyncCallbackHandler
When an `AgentMiddleware` hook (annotated with `hook_config(can_jump_to=["end"])`) returns `{"jump_to": "end", ...}`
the agent completes without invoking `AsyncCallbackHandler.on_agent_finish`.
Expected behavior: `on_agent_finish` should always be called as part of the agent lifecycle before the agent terminates (even when middleware directs a jump to "end").
After investigating this issue, I found that **`on_agent_finish` is not supported in the new `create_agent` API** - this is by design, not a bug.
`on_agent_finish` is not supported in the new `create_agent` API - this is by design, not a bug.
The new `create_agent` function (introduced in LangChain v1) is built on **LangGraph** and uses a **middleware-based architecture**
The classic callbacks like `on_agent_finish` and `on_agent_action` were designed for the legacy `AgentExecutor`
Use the `@after_agent` middleware hook instead, which is the equivalent lifecycle hook in the new architecture
Use the `@after_agent` middleware hook instead
If you specifically need `on_agent_finish` callbacks, you can use the classic `AgentExecutor` from `langchain-classic`
Unclear yet if we want to have these work together, these callbacks were meant to work with the very old `AgentExecutor` primitive...

Ability to identify and selectively stream only `AIMessage` tokens of the final answer to user's request in `updates` stream mode.
When using `astream` with `updates` streaming mode, I would like to be able to identify the *final* answer / response from the system to stream to the client of the agent streaming API endpoint.
However, streaming the LLM tokens is a challenge for this use case as there is no way to tell the context of the tokens spit out by the LLM and which LLM.
For inspiration, ollama chat has a `thinking` field in the stream which is used to show or hide reasoning traces before the final answer arrives.
I'm not sure it's possible to only stream tokens from the final user request.
Models can stream text and tool calls in the same turn (and typically text first).
Models can stream text and tool calls in the same turn (and typically text first).
AFAIK there's no way to know from the provider stream that tool calls are not going to follow.
ChatOpenAI drops reasoning_content field from reasoning models (o1, grok)
When using reasoning models (e.g., OpenAI o1, xAI Grok) via `ChatOpenAI`, the `reasoning_content` field returned by the Chat Completions API is silently dropped during response conversion.
When using reasoning models (e.g., OpenAI o1, xAI Grok)
The `reasoning_content` field should be preserved in `AIMessage.additional_kwargs`
The `reasoning_content` field is dropped in two places in `langchain_openai/chat_models/base.py`: 1. `_convert_dict_to_message()`
2. `_create_chat_result()`
## Reproduction
PR #34705 adds `reasoning_content` to the list of preserved fields in both locations.
Please bump the the latest (`1.1.7`) version.
We're actually on `langchain-openai==1.1.7` (the latest). (...) The bug exists on 1.1.7.
Yes, we use an OpenAI-compatible proxy (OCI Garthok), but it's a passthrough - doesn't modify responses.
Are you able to use `langchain-xai`? [ChatXAI](https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/integrations/chat/xai) just inherits BaseChatOpenAI and adds handling for these fields.
I considered `langchain-xai`, but it doesn't quite fit our use case. We use an OpenAI-compatible router that proxies requests to multiple providers
The fix is non-breaking and defensive: it only preserves `reasoning_content` if present, following the same pattern as `refusal` (which is already handled).

The field is in the raw response but gets dropped in `_create_chat_result()` because it's not in the list of fields copied to `additional_kwargs` (unlike `parsed` and `refusal` which are explicitly handled on lines 1504-1507). The field is already being received via `model_extra` - it's just being silently dropped.

evidence.quote: Tool artifact not in `on_tool_end` event when using `astream_events`

When using the `astream_events` API, it is unclear how to obtain the artifact returned by a tool. I would except it to be returned in the `on_tool_end` event, or maybe another event. But it is not in any event.

When using the `astream_events` API, it is unclear how to obtain the artifact returned by a tool. I would except it to be returned in the `on_tool_end` event, or maybe another event. But it is not in any event.

A solution I have found is to emit a custom event directly from the tool, using `adispatch_custom_event()`. While this solution work fine, I still imagine not being able to use artifact for that is not intended.

I understand the desire to keep the base class lean, but I'd gently argue that's precisely why this belongs here - `BaseChatOpenAI` is a primitive that the ecosystem builds on. Asking every router/proxy user to create their own subclass fragments the ecosystem and reduces the value of having a shared primitive.

Title: .with_structured_output() causes tool_choice 422 error with Azure-hosted Deepseek V3

I'm trying to use the langchain library to invoke a structured output model using .with_structured_output() with an Azure-hosted Deepseek V3 deployment. ... Instead, it raises a 422 UnprocessableEntityError
raises a 422 UnprocessableEntityError saying tool_choice must be a string, but it was passed as an object: {'type': 'function', 'function': {'name': 'ChatbotOutput'}}
This works as expected on: ✅ OpenAI GPT-4.1
This works as expected on: ✅ Azure GPT-4.1-mini
This works as expected on: ✅ Deepseek V3 using Deepseek provided APIs
I found the issue. It's an incompatibility issue.
Until then, you can try this workaround
.with_structured_output(ChatbotOutput, method='function_calling')
.bind(tool_choice='required')
I will create a PR to fix it.
Can you share your messages that you are passing?

Any update?
